_notes: Each entry groups goals by the energy source section they appear under in the 2022 SEP (electricity / naturalGas / gasoline / diesel / communityIncentives). Goal/Strategy/Action numbering is preserved verbatim from the source (e.g. a source typo duplicating 'STRATEGY 2.1' twice under Water Resources Goal 2 is corrected to 2.1/2.2 here, noted inline). statusUpdate2025 is null with dataGap:true where the 2025 Progress Report did not address that specific goal.

progress2025.note: Increase since 2019, against a 40% reduction target for 2025.

goals.text: Reduce water demand by 20% per capita by 2030.
goals.evaluationMethod: Track yearly water usage and normalize per capita. Compare to previous year normalized water usage for change in usage. Usage per capita should drop by an average of at least 1% yearly.

actions.text: Audit all City buildings for water efficiency opportunities.
Audit and fix distribution leaks.
Educate citizens on the importance of water conservation.
Develop incentives for citizens to reduce personal water demand.
Establish funding to assist vulnerable residents to reduce water demand.
Perform a study of other cities' water pricing structures.

goals.evaluationMethod: Track and report electricity consumption in kWh at the end of each year and compare to previous year for change in usage.

actions.text: Audit water and wastewater equipment.
Implement efficiency measures suggested by audit.
Monitor equipment to ensure efficiency levels are met.

strategies.sourceLabelNote: Printed in source as 'STRATEGY 2.1' a second time; action IDs (A2.2.x) make clear this is Strategy 2.2.
strategies.text: Increase equipment efficiency (J. Edward Kitchen Operations Center).

actions.text: Perform an energy audit on J. Edward Kitchen Operations Center.
Implement efficiency measures suggested by audit.
Monitor buildings to ensure efficiency levels are met.

statusUpdate2025.statusNote: Unlike most other goals in this dataset, the 2025 Progress Report describes activity for this goal (GSO WaterWise Portal, kit distribution) without attaching one of its usual status words (Complete/Ongoing/In Progress/Initiated). Reported with no explicit status label — represent this as its own state in the UI, not silently mapped to 'Ongoing'.
statusUpdate2025.text: Preliminary figures put electricity use by the Water Resources Department at 69,845 MWh in 2025, a 2.0% increase since 2019. In May, the department introduced the GSO WaterWise Portal, supported by the City's system-wide upgrade to smart meters, offering customized usage alerts, near real-time water use data, and personalized conservation tips.

fundingLink.note: EECBG funds the water/energy conservation kit outreach program distributed via GSO WaterWise.

goals.text: Meet total demand with renewable energy by 2040.
goals.evaluationMethod: Track and report the amount of electricity generated by renewable sources for Water Resources.

strategies.text: Install 1 MW ground PV solar project at T. Z. Osborne.

actions.text: Secure funding for project.
RFP developed/bids secured/contract awarded.
Identify suitable land for array (5 acres for a 1 MW array).
Identify suitable land for smaller arrays (>1MW).
Conduct feasibility study of solar installation on existing parking decks.
Determine cost effectiveness of the project.
Investigate and resolve previously identified federal permitting issues.
If A3.3.1 is positive, RFP developed/bids secured/contract awarded.

strategies.text: Research electricity generation by capturing waste heat from bio-solid incineration.

actions.text: Feasibility study to determine compatibility with existing equipment.
Determine cost effectiveness of the project if A3.4.1 is positive.
If A3.4.2 is positive, RFP developed/bids secured/contract awarded.

relatedExternalFinding.text: Neither SEP document mentions it, but a City-owned-land renewable asset roughly 5x larger than this goal's own 1 MW T.Z. Osborne proposal (Strategy 3.1, never built) was actually constructed elsewhere in the City during this same window: a 4.8 MW solar array at the White Street Landfill, operational since October 2020. It's just attributed to a different department (Field Operations / Solid Waste & Recycling), not Water Resources — the SEP's department-siloed accounting has no mechanism to credit it here.
relatedExternalFinding.framingNote: Stated factually, not as an accusation: this is a gap in the reports' own department-based accounting structure, not evidence Water Resources underperformed relative to what it controls.

citation.title: White Street Landfill Solar Facility (Legistar File 15-0607)
citation.note: Not referenced in either SEP source document — found via independent research.

departments.name: Transportation Department (Electricity)
departments.note2025Reorg: The Greensboro Transit Agency (GTA) was a division of the Transportation Department (GDOT) until 2023, when it became a separate department. 2025 progress figures below are reported as 'Transportation & Transit Departments' combined, which limits direct comparability to the 2019 baseline (which predates the GTA split).

progress2025.note: Combined Transportation & Transit electricity use; decline is partly a reporting-boundary effect of the 2023 GTA split, not purely efficiency gains.

goals.text: Increase LED streetlamp conversion by 5% per year.
goals.evaluationMethod: Track and report the increase in streetlamps converted to LEDs at the end of each year.

strategies.text: Increase the conversion of street lighting to LED.

actions.text: Work with Duke Energy to ensure conversion meets the yearly goal.

statusUpdate2025.text: GDOT works with Duke Energy to coordinate LED conversion of the company's more than 27,400 streetlamps and other public outdoor lighting units. LED streetlamp conversion data for 2025 (percent converted) was not available for inclusion in this report. However, total energy consumption by streetlamps dropped from 18,276 MWh in 2022 to 1,952 MWh in 2025 — a reduction of 89.3%.
statusUpdate2025.dataGapNote: 2025 LED conversion percentage explicitly stated as unavailable in the source report — a self-acknowledged gap.
statusUpdate2025.streetlampBaselineYearNote: This comparison uses 2022 as the baseline, not 2019 — different reference year than the SEP's 2007/2019 baselines.

fundingLink.note: Duke Energy-driven infrastructure conversion; no named City grant or contract identified in the source.

departments.name: Buildings Managed by Engineering & Inspections
departments.note: This is a functional 'Buildings' use-type grouping (32% of total City electricity, per Table 3/Figure 5) covering City buildings across many departments — distinct from the tiny 'Engineering & Inspections' department line item in Table 3 (266,700 kWh, its own office usage only). Do not conflate the two when charting.

statusUpdate2025.text: Preliminary figures put total electricity use in the City's buildings at 45,576 MWh in 2025, a 1.0% increase since 2019. OSR contracted with Systems Contractors, Inc. (SCI) to audit the City's 10-highest energy-using buildings and recommend energy conservation measures. Level 1 audits identified needed maintenance and corrective measures; SCI suggested more-detailed Level 2 audits. OSR is working to carry out these projects and identify needed funding. In October, OSR encouraged staff to coordinate major energy-consuming activities with Duke Energy's non-summer, seasonal time-of-use electricity rates.

fundingLink.note: SCI's Level 1/2 building audits are the named contract behind this goal's 2025 progress.

statusUpdate2025.text: The Central Library's new roof was installed in 2025. OSR is identifying funding to execute the needed maintenance and corrective measures identified through the library's Level 1 building energy audit.

fundingLink.note: The cited Level 1 building energy audit is SCI's; the roof installation itself is not tied to a named funding source in the source documents (OSR is 'identifying funding' for remaining corrective measures).

statusUpdate2025.text: The Police Department purchased 32 new, hybrid Ford Explorer Interceptors to replace existing internal combustion vehicles, and intends to replace its prisoner transport van with an electric vehicle (EV). OSR staff works with the Equipment Services Division to identify opportunities to replace the least-efficient vehicles with hybrid or EV models where practical.

fundingLink.note: Vehicle purchases described as routine fleet replacement via Equipment Services Division; no named grant or contract stated in the source.

strategies.text: Support implementation of GTA Mobility Greensboro 2040 Plan to increase ridership throughout the City.

actions.text: Identify opportunities to support the implementation of the plan to increase ridership.

statusUpdate2025.text: Implementation of GTA's zero-emissions fleet transition plan is in progress. GTA purchased three new, Gillig battery-electric buses (one 40-foot model and two 35-foot models) and two new 60-kilowatt Heliox portable chargers, anticipated to help reduce annual diesel consumption.

fundingLink.correctionNote: Step 2 marked this goal unfunded because neither SEP PDF names a funding source for GTA's bus/charger purchases. Independent research (Step 3) found five real, externally-verified grants dating back to 2016 funding this fleet — this was a data-layer gap in the source documents, not an absence of funding.
fundingLink.note: Most significant: the $22.4M FTA Low-No grant (July 2024) is publicly credited by Greensboro's Transit Director to the 2022 Strategic Energy Plan — see funding.json > externalGrants > grants > fta-low-no-2024 > quote. All five citations are sourceType:'external' — none of this funding history appears in either SEP source document.

additionalRecommendedActions: Economic Development & Business Services Office (EDBS) - collaborate with the Nussbaum Center for Entrepreneurship to incubate new local green businesses.
EDBS - offer economic development assistance grants/forgivable loans to green businesses relocating to, or expanding in, Greensboro.
EDBS - offer grants/forgivable loans to businesses that implement energy saving or emissions reducing improvements to their facilities.

Housing and Neighborhood Development Department - offer grants/forgivable loans to low- and moderate-income homeowners and affordable housing property owners who implement energy saving or emissions reducing improvements.
Sustainability Office - collaborate with Libraries to organize an annual Neighborhood Sustainability Challenge Program.
Sustainability Office - establish funding and collaborate with CSC to formally launch the Greensboro Responsible Business Awards Program.
goals.statusUpdate2025Note: Not directly updated by number in the 2025 report, but Recommendation 3 / Strategy 3.3 (outreach to impacted/vulnerable communities via the equity prioritization tool) and the EECBG-funded water/energy conservation kits address closely related ground. See recommendations.json Recommendation 3 and departments.json Water Resources Goal 2.

fundingLink.note: No named grant/contract directly tied to this goal itself; adjacent EECBG-funded conservation kit work sits under Water Resources Goal 2 instead.

departments.name: White Street Landfill (Field Operations / Solid Waste & Recycling)
departments.note: This entry exists entirely outside the SEP's goal structure — it is not derived from either SEP source document, has no Goal/Strategy/Action numbering, and no 'goals' array. It was added because independent research (Legistar, Step 3) surfaced a real, City-related renewable energy asset with no home anywhere in the SEP's department-by-department accounting. Field Operations was renamed 'Solid Waste & Recycling' as of the 2025 Progress Report (see recommendations.json > REC-1 > status2025) — the White Street Landfill is that department's facility.

crossReferencedFrom.note: See that goal's relatedExternalFinding for the framing: the SEP's own accounting treats Water Resources' renewable progress as stalled (its 1 MW T.Z. Osborne proposal never happened), while a City-related asset roughly 5x larger was built in the same window under a different department, uncredited in either report. A factual gap in department-siloed accounting, not an accusation.

externalAssets.name: White Street Landfill Solar Facility
externalAssets.capacityConfidence: medium — see funding.json > externallyVerifiedRenewableAssets > whiteStreetLandfillSolar > asBuilt for full confidence notes and a homes-equivalent figure discrepancy (65 vs. 650) not resolved in this research pass.
externalAssets.acreage: 15-20 acres as authorized (2015); ~30 acres as commonly reported once built — discrepancy not resolved to a single primary source.
externalAssets.operator: Petra Engineering (2015 authorization) / 'ReNew Petra' (current common name) — plausibly a rebrand, not independently confirmed.
externalAssets.financingModel: Land lease / independent power producer — NOT a Solar Power Purchase Agreement (City does not purchase the power; developer sells to Duke Energy directly, City receives ~$6,000/year lease rent plus a purchase option after year 7). See funding.json for the full distinction.

citation.title: Resolution Authorizing an Amendment to the White Street Landfill Gas Utilization Project Agreement with Petra Engineering to Include a Solar Facility
citation.note: Not referenced in either SEP source document — found via independent research.

externalAssets.name: White Street Landfill Gas-to-Energy Project
externalAssets.note: Separate project under the same amended agreement as the solar facility; converts landfill methane to electricity.
externalAssets.homesEquivalentConfidence: medium — see funding.json > externallyVerifiedRenewableAssets > whiteStreetLandfillGasToEnergy for confidence notes.

citation.publisher: City of Greensboro (OSR 'Solar Energy Central' page, via search-result aggregation)
citation.title: Solar Energy Central
citation.note: Direct fetch returned HTTP 403; figure drawn from search-engine summaries referencing this page. Not referenced in either SEP source document.

fundingLink.note: Privately financed (land lease / IPP model, not a City grant) — 'funded' here means the project happened and is documented, not that City grant dollars built it.
